crime.value: Kidnapping/disappearance of 6-year-old Madeline Shultz from her bedroom; question: how did she vanish and who took her?
crime.quote: their 6 year old daughter, Madeline Shultz, was taken on December 22nd by an unknown assailant

victim.value: Madeline Shultz, 6-year-old daughter of wealthy couple, targeted due to mistaken identity - resembles another missing child
victim.quote: Mara and Madeline look almost identical, with the exception of the mole right next to Madeline's right eye

closed_world.value: The Shultz household and estate with secret tunnels, limited to family members and household staff
closed_world.quote: stay in the secrete tunnels, only coming out when Madeline knew it was safe

culprit_and_motive.value: Charlotte (the maid) kidnapped Madeline believing she was her own missing daughter Mara due to their identical appearance
culprit_and_motive.quote: You thought that the Shultz had taken your daughter when they came home from Paris and lied

resolution.value: Alice Bellicose deduces the truth through investigation and confronts Charlotte, revealing both girls were hiding together
resolution.quote: Alice said. 'Right then. That's enough drama for one day. It's almost Christmas!'

investigator.value: Alice Bellicose, amateur detective who takes over the case from the official Detective Greene
investigator.quote: Oscar Quinces sat the newspaper down on the table and looked over at his friend, Alice Bellicose

suspect_architecture.value: Five household suspects with varied access: Charlotte the maid (motive: mistaken identity of daughter), Pierre the groundskeeper (key holder), Grover (key access), Frances the cook (kitchen access), plus Detective Greene as official investigator
suspect_architecture.quote: Charlotte said honestly. "Well, we've done all we can here," Alice said, and without warning

red_herring.value: The open window and traditional kidnapping theory - Charlotte admits leaving window open and closing it before officials arrived, misdirecting from the truth that Madeline hid voluntarily in secret tunnels
red_herring.quote: "I left her window open," "Yes, I closed it because I was afraid to admit what I had done,"

clue_fairness.value: Missing bread rolls mentioned early (Frances made three, gone by morning), Charlotte's familiar appearance to Alice, and the physical similarity between Madeline and Mara all presented before solution
clue_fairness.quote: "I made three, fresh rolls of bread," Frances said "And they were no longer there"

social_world.value: Wealthy employer-servant hierarchy in 1950s London household - the Shultz family as employers with staff including maid, cook, groundskeeper, creating access patterns and power dynamics around the missing child
social_world.quote: "I was afraid Mrs. Shultz would hate me," "Oh, don't tell her," Charlotte pleaded

alibi.value: Pierre claims he never saw Grover and has his own engraved keys, Frances was in kitchen all day until 7:54 p.m., Charlotte admits to opening/closing window but hides true motive
alibi.quote: "I can prove it is mine for I engrave all of my possessions with my initials, PI."

reveal_mechanic.value: Physical resemblance recognition - Alice notices Charlotte looks familiar, leading to discovery that Charlotte mistook Madeline for her own missing daughter Mara due to their near-identical appearance except for a mole
reveal_mechanic.quote: "You look familiar," Alice said to Charlotte. "Have we met?"

media_and_audience.value: Newspaper coverage frames it as tragic Christmas kidnapping, Detective Greene takes public credit for solving the case despite Alice actually solving it, shaping public perception of both crime and resolution
media_and_audience.quote: "Detective Greene took the credit, of course, for the finding of Madeline Shultz and Mara."

_meta.title: ebook ren willi the dissappearance fo madeline shultz
